slides.script:
Good morning, everyone. My name is Chris Wu, and today I’m presenting my thesis: 'Physical-World Observation for Personal AI Agent Personalization.' [Pause] 

Before we dive into the details, I want you to imagine a scenario. Imagine you have a personal biographer who knows everything about you—your schedule, your emails, and your preferences. 

But there’s a catch: this biographer has never actually met you. Despite their immense intelligence, they still feel like a stranger. 

That’s because they understand you only through your digital traces; they’ve never actually witnessed your world. They know you bought a book, but they didn't see you place it on a high shelf where it’s remained untouched. They know you have a meeting, but they don't see the intensity of your focus as you prepare for it. 

This is the state of AI agent personalization today. My research aims to bridge this gap—exploring how we can enable agents to understand us physically, situatedly, and holistically.

slides.subtitle: The Claw
slides.script: I call this 'The Claw.' It represents the defining shift in the AI landscape: the transition from assistants that merely talk, to agents that actually act. 'The Claw' is the symbol of digital agency—the ability for a system to reach out and manipulate your computer, navigating your files, executing code, and managing your workspace autonomously.

slides.subtitle: Personal AI Agents are Here
slides.script:
We are at a turning point, moving from 'AI assistants' to 'AI agents'. 

Personal AI agents represent a shift from reactive chatbots to intelligent systems that deeply understand you and proactively act on your behalf. 

On the left is the traditional AI Assistant: reactive, session-based, and focused on transient, immediate tasks. 

On the right is the Personal AI Agent, proactive and maintain continuous context, and can track long-term work and projects. 

This creates a fundamentally different relationship where the agent 'never forgets who you are.'

slides.subtitle: The OpenClaw Fever
slides.script: We are witnessing a global 'OpenClaw fever'. From Shenzhen to New York, thousands of people are gathering just to learn how to run this new kind of intelligence on their own machines.

slides.subtitle: What is OpenClaw
slides.script:
So, what is OpenClaw? 

As this architecture shows, OpenClaw acts as the orchestrator between your User Intentions and the Large Language Models. 

While models like Claude or GPT provide the 'brain,' OpenClaw manages your Memory, Tasks, and Computer Context locally on your machine. 

It is the persistent 'glue' that gives the AI ability to 
1. manipulate your digital environment.
2. work with cross-session consistency

slides.subtitle: OpenClaw's Spreading
slides.script:
This is a global deployment spreading at hyper-velocity. 

We are seeing massive clusters exceeding 50,000 active instances in both China and the US.

It's literally blankets the planet.

slides.subtitle: OpenClaw's GitHub Stars Growth
slides.script:
The data confirms the revolution. 

OpenClaw is the fastest-growing open-source project in history, hitting 100,000 stars in less than a month and overtaking industry giants like React in record time. The scale of this growth is simply historic.

slides.subtitle: Personalization
slides.script:
But first — what do we even mean by personalization?

Blom defined it as changing a system to increase its personal relevance to the individual. My framing: the service fits, or better yet, exceeds, your expectation.

Think of it as a spectrum — from systems that barely notice you exist, to AI that adapts in real time, all the way to human conversation, which is continuous personalization at every single moment. That's the bar we're aiming for.

slides.subtitle: Why Personalization Matters
slides.script:
So why does this matter so much for an AI agent specifically?

Look at the left side. This is a generic agent — one that doesn't know you. Every task has to pass through a fixed prompt bottleneck. Your context, your preferences, your goals — they leak out. The output scatters. It might be technically correct, but it rarely hits what you actually needed.

Now look at the right side. A personalized agent fuses your preferences, your history, your workflows into a single continuous context. The same task comes in — and it reaches the intended outcome, every time.

This is what I mean when I say: Personalization is Effectiveness. It's not a nice-to-have feature. It is the difference between an agent that works and one that doesn't.

slides.subtitle: The Blind Spot
slides.script: However, there is a massive 'Blind Spot.'

slides.subtitle: Two Worlds
slides.script:
This blind spot exists because we inhabit two worlds simultaneously. 

On the left is the Digital Dimension—the realm of browser tabs, calendar invites, and chat logs that our agents know perfectly. 

But on the right is the Physical World—where our actual lives unfold. This is where an agent could finally see that book gathering dust on your shelf, or recognize your stress levels in the clutter of your desk. 

Currently, our agents are personalizing from only half the picture. They understand the digital trace, but they are strangers to the human context.

slides.subtitle: Research Landscape
slides.script:
Existing research has focused on lifelogging or activity recognition, but rarely on how these physical observations can be integrated into the core memory of a persistent AI agent. 

We are moving beyond just 'tracking' to 'integrating' physical context into the agent's cognition.

slides.subtitle: Research Questions
slides.script: My research addresses three core questions: First, what unique personalization data becomes visible through egocentric vision? Second, when does this information actually improve agent performance? And third, what are the design requirements for a memory system that can handle this continuous physical input?

slides.subtitle: Design Rationale
slides.script: Let's dive into the Design Rationale.

slides.subtitle: Why Egocentric Vision?
slides.script:
We chose egocentric vision because it captures the world from the user's perspective. 

It doesn't just record video; it records the user's 'implicit attention.' What you look at, what you interact with, and how you move through space are powerful signals of your current state and long-term habits.

slides.subtitle: Why OpenClaw?
slides.script: OpenClaw was selected for its 'transparent memory' architecture. By using local markdown files, it ensures that sensitive physical-world data remains under the user's control. It allows us to build a memory system that is human-readable, auditable, and entirely private.

slides.subtitle: How OpenClaw works
slides.script: The core of OpenClaw is its ability to synthesize discrete notes into a long-term knowledge base. Our system leverages this by feeding structured physical observations directly into this existing knowledge loop.

slides.subtitle: System Design
slides.script: I'd like to walk you through the system design of SpaceSelfLog—a full-stack pipeline designed to turn raw egocentric video into high-level agent intelligence.

slides.subtitle: The Pipeline
slides.script: The pipeline is organized into four distinct layers: Capture, Preprocessing, Inference, and Memory Integration. This modular approach allows us to handle high-volume visual data on a resource-constrained mobile device while performing heavy semantic reasoning on the server.

slides.subtitle: Layer 1 Capture & Layer 1.5 Preprocess
slides.script: To manage battery life and privacy, we use 'Adaptive Capture.' Sensors like VAD and IMU detect changes in the user's state—like starting a conversation or moving to a new room—and automatically trigger a higher frame rate. Preprocessing then filters out blurs and performs local deduplication to ensure only high-signal keyframes are processed.

slides.subtitle: Capture Monitor
slides.script: This is our real-time capture monitor, where you can see the adaptive interval strategy in action. The system becomes 'attentive' exactly when the physical environment changes, ensuring we capture transitions without wasting computation on static scenes.

slides.subtitle: Layer 2 Inference
slides.script: In the Inference layer, keyframes are sent to a Vision-Language Model. The model is prompted not just to describe the scene, but to interpret it: 'What does this reveal about the user's habits?' It extracts activities, locations, objects, and social context into structured JSON.

slides.subtitle: Layer 3 Memory
slides.script: This is the core innovation: our 3-tier memory architecture. 'Physical Logs' store the raw sensory data; 'Physical Insights' provide a rolling intra-day distillation of noteworthy events; and 'Physical Pattern' maintains the user's long-term identity. This tiered approach mimics human memory—from immediate sensory perception to long-term behavioral abstraction.

slides.subtitle: Server Monitor
slides.script: The server monitor tracks the flow of these inferences and manages the nightly consolidation of daily insights into the persistent pattern profile. It's the engine that powers the agent's long-term understanding.

slides.subtitle: Layer 3.5 Retrieval
slides.script: Finally, the retrieval layer allows the agent to query this memory using semantic search. Whether the agent needs a specific detail from an hour ago or a general habit from last week, the memory is indexed and ready.

slides.subtitle: Full Pipeline Overview
slides.script: From the wearable device to the structured memory file, this end-to-end pipeline enables a seamless bridge between a user's physical actions and their agent's digital reasoning.

slides.subtitle: Study Design
slides.script: To evaluate this architecture, I conducted a two-week autoethnographic study using a 'Research through Design' methodology.

slides.subtitle: Study Design
slides.script: The study was divided into two phases: a Calibration phase to tune the pipeline parameters, followed by a formal Observation phase where all system parameters were frozen to ensure the integrity of the collected data.

slides.subtitle: Data Collection
slides.script: We collected a comprehensive dataset including thousands of egocentric keyframes, full interaction transcripts with the agent, and daily structured journals to capture the interpretive nuances of the system's performance.

slides.subtitle: Experimental Protocol
slides.script: The study protocol involved three observation methods: Passive Observation of natural interactions, Structured Probes to test specific memory recall, and a Proactive Cron job that allowed the agent to initiate contact based on its own judgment of recent physical events.

slides.subtitle: Study Device
slides.script: The study device—a head-mounted iPhone proxy—approximates the perspective of upcoming smart glasses, capturing what the user actually looks at to maintain the 'implicit attention' signal central to our design.

slides.subtitle: Design Iteration Log
slides.script: Every modification made during the calibration phase was logged, documenting the iterative process of balancing capture density, VLM accuracy, and memory retention.

slides.subtitle: Autoethnographic Journal
slides.script: My journals captured the 'lived experience' of the system—identifying moments where the agent successfully anticipated a need versus moments where the physical context was misunderstood or ignored.

slides.subtitle: Results
slides.script: Now, let's look at what this data reveals about the potential of physical-world observation for personalization.

slides.subtitle: Full Timeline Wall (1/6)
slides.script: This 'Timeline Wall' represents two weeks of my life through the eyes of the agent. It captures the sheer diversity and density of information that egocentric perception makes visible—from morning routines to late-night work sessions—creating a visual tapestry of my personalized user model.

slides.subtitle: Activity Daily Rhythm Matrix
slides.script: The Activity Rhythm Matrix reveals the deep structure of my life. By logging physical activities over time, the agent can identify recurring routines and deviations, allowing it to adapt its suggestions to the specific rhythm of my day.

slides.subtitle: Object Co-occurrence Network
slides.script: This network graph shows how objects 'cluster' around certain activities. It illustrates my habits: which tools I use for work, which ingredients I use for cooking, and how these physical objects define the context of my needs.

slides.subtitle: Context to Activity Flow
slides.script: The Sankey diagram shows the flow between environments and actions. It proves that physical location is a primary driver of behavior, and by understanding this flow, the agent can better predict and support user intent.

slides.subtitle: Apartment Activity Heatmap
slides.script: By projecting activities onto a floor plan, we identify the 'hotspots' of my life. These aren't just coordinates; they are the physical anchors of my identity, which the agent can now use to ground its responses in spatial reality.

slides.subtitle: Integrated Spatial Activity Map
slides.script: Finally, this Integrated Map represents the ultimate vision: a personal AI agent that is no longer blind. By bridging the physical and digital worlds, we have created an agent that doesn't just manage your data—it understands your life. This is the future of truly personal intelligence. Thank you.
